name: San Antonio, Texas
tagline: The 7th-largest U.S. city and the anchor of 'Cyber City USA' — San Antonio runs city technology through its Information Technology Services Department (ITSD), sits at the center of a federal and state cyber cluster (NSA Texas, 16th Air Force/AFCYBER, the new Texas Cyber Command), and adopted a ~$4B FY2026 budget with $130M+ in technology funding.
overview: The City of San Antonio is the government of the 7th-largest city in the United States, serving roughly 1.5 million residents and operating an adopted FY2026 budget of about $4 billion. City technology is run by the Information Technology Services Department (ITSD), which provides IT services across more than 40 departments and 500-plus city facilities — spanning cybersecurity, network infrastructure, enterprise applications, end-user support, public-safety systems, data analytics, smart-city strategy, and digital transformation. San Antonio is widely branded 'Cyber City USA': it holds the largest concentration of cyber assets in the country outside the Washington, D.C. region, home to NSA Texas (about 3,000 employees), the 16th Air Force (AFCYBER) at Joint Base San Antonio, and now the new Texas Cyber Command — the nation's largest state-run cybersecurity agency, headquartered in San Antonio at UT San Antonio. For a technology seller this is a large municipal IT buyer embedded in a dense federal/state cyber ecosystem, with fresh IT and security leadership in 2026 (a new CIO and CISO) and a city budget that put $130M+ behind technology. San Antonio buys through its own central purchasing process and cooperative vehicles, including the Texas DIR Cooperative Contracts.

budget.intro: San Antonio adopted a balanced ~$4B FY2026 budget that allocates more than $130M to technology-related initiatives. The Information Technology Services Department (ITSD) itself is funded at about $94M, with additional technology dollars flowing through the capital improvement program and the Office of Innovation rather than ITSD alone.

stats.label: Total city budget (FY26)
stats.note: Adopted, balanced FY2026 budget across general fund, enterprise, and restricted funds

stats.label: ITSD budget (FY26)
stats.note: Largest technology allocation; covers enterprise apps, cybersecurity, network, end-user support, public-safety IT, data analytics

stats.label: Tech-related funding (FY26)
stats.note: ITSD plus ~$32M citywide tech infrastructure (CIP, part of a 6-yr ~$167M modernization), ~$3M department IT capital, and ~$1.5M Office of Innovation

notes: The ~$32M citywide technology infrastructure line sits within the capital improvement program and is described as part of a broader six-year, ~$167M modernization initiative — a multi-year infrastructure refresh signal.
The Office of Innovation (~$1.5M) funds smart-city programs, digital inclusion, and the city's Research & Development League — separate from ITSD's operating budget but part of the technology agenda.

initiatives.name: Cyber City USA ecosystem and Texas Cyber Command
initiatives.summary: San Antonio is the national cyber hub outside D.C., home to NSA Texas (~3,000 employees), the 16th Air Force (AFCYBER) at Joint Base San Antonio, and the new Texas Cyber Command — the largest state-run cyber agency, launched at UT San Antonio in 2025 and led by retired Vice Adm. Timothy 'T.J.' White, scaling toward ~130 staff. The city benefits from and partners with this cluster; understand the federal/state/city seams before forecasting security spend.

initiatives.name: Citywide technology modernization (6-year CIP)
initiatives.summary: The FY2026 budget funds ~$32M of citywide technology infrastructure within a broader six-year, ~$167M modernization initiative, plus ~$3M in department-level IT capital for public safety, libraries, and administration. A multi-year refresh track for infrastructure, network, and end-user compute across 40+ departments and 500+ facilities.

initiatives.name: SmartSA smart-city program
initiatives.summary: SmartSA — a consortium of the City, CPS Energy, and local partners run through the Office of Innovation — has executed 30-plus pilots and projects since 2018 using a people-centered model, including smart streetlight sensor deployments with CPS Energy. A live lane for IoT, data platforms, edge, and connected-infrastructure use cases.

initiatives.name: Enterprise cybersecurity and ARSOC
initiatives.summary: ITSD runs city cybersecurity, including the Alamo Regional Security Operations Center (ARSOC), now directed by new CISO Shannon Lawson. With fresh security leadership and a regional SOC mission, there is active appetite for security operations, endpoint, network security, and risk-visibility tooling across the city estate.

procurement.intro: San Antonio buys two main ways, and a seller should confirm both. It runs its own central purchasing process (formal/informal solicitations, vendor registration) and it uses cooperative contracts — including the Texas DIR Cooperative Contracts — to buy technology at pre-negotiated statewide pricing without running a separate RFP.

vehicles.name: City of San Antonio central purchasing
vehicles.detail: The city's Finance/Purchasing function manages vendor registration and competitive solicitations (RFPs/RFCSPs, bids). The path for city-specific competitive procurements; confirm Dell's registration and the relevant commodity categories.

vehicles.name: Texas DIR Cooperative Contracts
vehicles.detail: As a Texas local government, San Antonio is an eligible DIR co-op customer and can buy hardware, software, and services off pre-competed statewide contracts. Confirm Dell's DIR coverage is active and correctly scoped — the fastest catalog-style path.

vehicles.name: Other cooperative contracts
vehicles.detail: San Antonio, like most large Texas cities, also leverages national/cooperative purchasing vehicles for catalog goods and services. Confirm which co-ops Dell holds and which the city actively buys through for a given category.

powerStructure.name: Gina Ortiz Jones
powerStructure.title: Mayor
powerStructure.note: Elected in June 2025 as San Antonio's first new mayor in years; a former Air Force intelligence officer and ex-Under Secretary of the Air Force. Sets political priorities and joined Bloomberg Philanthropies' Mayors AI Forum in 2026, signaling an AI/technology-forward agenda. In a council-manager city the mayor drives policy direction while the City Manager runs operations.

powerStructure.name: Erik Walsh
powerStructure.title: City Manager
powerStructure.note: Leads city operations, organizational development, budget, fiscal policy, and contracts, with unilateral authority to sign contracts up to $2.5M. The top of the administrative house — the executive owning budget priorities and the SmartSA partnership direction; presented the FY2027 budget amid a projected two-year deficit.

powerStructure.name: Marshall Ramsey
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Officer (ITSD)
powerStructure.note: Named CIO in May 2026, succeeding Craig Hopkins (CIO 2017-2026). Prior CIO at LA County Public Health and Santa Barbara County; UT Austin computer-science background. The primary IT relationship and owner of citywide technology and digital transformation.

powerStructure.name: Shannon Lawson
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Security Officer & ARSOC Director
powerStructure.note: Reports to the CIO; also directs the Alamo Regional Security Operations Center. Prior assistant CIO/CISO for Phoenix and CISO/CTO for the State of Alaska. Owns the city's security strategy, policy, and SOC operations.

powerStructure.name: Texas Cyber Command (TJ White)
powerStructure.title: State cyber agency, San Antonio HQ
powerStructure.note: Not a city office, but a major adjacent buying center and partner — the largest state-run cyber agency, HQ'd in San Antonio at UTSA, scaling staff and capabilities. Track for separate state-funded security demand in the same metro.

news.title: City Manager Walsh presents FY2027 budget amid projected ~$158M two-year deficit; first property-tax-rate increase proposed in 33 years
news.impact: Slowed growth and a ~2.1% drop in taxable values push the city toward the maximum tax increase allowed without a vote plus early cost cuts (delayed police hires, fee increases). A tighter fiscal window pressures discretionary IT spend — lead with cost-optimization, consolidation, and as-a-service TCO framing; the budget is finalized in September.

news.title: San Antonio advances an AI Integration Strategy and Mayor Jones joins Bloomberg's Mayors AI Forum
news.impact: Council (CCR from CM Whyte) pushes a citywide AI strategy for public services, efficiency, and public safety, under Texas's new TRAIGA AI-disclosure law; the city already pilots Microsoft Copilot and AI for contract review and translation. An opening for governed AI infrastructure, data-platform, and secure-compute positioning tied to ITSD and the Office of Innovation.

news.title: Marshall Ramsey named San Antonio CIO, succeeding retiring Craig Hopkins
news.impact: New IT leadership over a 40+ department, 500+ facility estate. Re-introduce Dell and rebuild the top IT relationship; new CIOs often reassess platforms and partners early.

news.title: Texas Cyber Command formally stands up in San Antonio under Vice Adm. (Ret.) T.J. White
news.impact: Largest state-run cyber agency anchors in the metro, scaling toward ~130 staff. A new state-funded security buying center adjacent to the city account — map it separately.

news.title: Shannon Lawson named San Antonio CISO and ARSOC director
news.impact: Experienced security leader (ex-Phoenix, ex-Alaska) now owns the city's security agenda and regional SOC. Build the CISO relationship for security-operations, endpoint, and risk-tooling motions.

news.title: Council adopts balanced ~$4B FY2026 budget with $130M+ in technology funding
news.impact: ITSD funded at ~$94M plus ~$32M citywide tech infrastructure (part of a 6-yr ~$167M modernization). Confirms multi-year infrastructure and modernization spend — an entry point for ISG, security, and services.

dellActions: Re-introduce Dell to new CIO Marshall Ramsey (named May 2026) and new CISO Shannon Lawson — fresh leadership over a 40+ department, 500+ facility estate is the moment to reset the account relationship and platform conversation.

Lead with infrastructure and modernization: position ISG, network, end-user compute, and managed services against the ~$32M FY26 citywide technology line and the six-year, ~$167M modernization initiative.

Bring municipal security-operations and endpoint offers tied to the CISO/ARSOC mission — San Antonio's 'Cyber City USA' identity and regional SOC make security a credibility-forward, reference-driven motion.

Map the adjacent Texas Cyber Command (state-funded, San Antonio HQ at UTSA) as a separate buying center — same metro, different budget and leadership — and coordinate coverage so city and state cyber demand aren't conflated.

Confirm Dell's DIR Cooperative Contract coverage (San Antonio is an eligible DIR co-op buyer) and verify standing in City of San Antonio central purchasing for catalog and competitive buys; bring SmartSA-relevant IoT/data references for the smart-city lane.
